author: @YodaWhat
text: You only have to exceed the normal speed if you are trying to generate your normal powerline frequency. If you are just going to rectify the output, like for charging batteries or running an inverter, then you can spin the motor-cum-generator at any convenient speed. The maximum power output increases with the RPM you drive the generator. On small units, you can greatly exceed the speed it would have when used as a normal motor. That will make the frequency and voltage go up, while the maximum output current remains about constant, because it is limited by the current required to produce _magnetic saturation_ of the steel laminations. But excessive speed or load will tend to make the generator run hot, and possibly fail if it gets too hot.

Edward's EZ Equilibrium Rotation Law
"In a closed system, rotational equilibrium is achieved when the sum of torques acting on the system about any axis is zero. This principle applies universally across physical systems, maintaining re lance and stability in rotational motion."
This law states that in any closed system, the rotational equilibrium is maintained when the total torque (the rotational force) acting on the system around any chosen axis is balanced, ensuring steady rotational motion without acceleration or deceleration.

author: @joehigdon9847
text: I live in NE Alabama and back in the 90s we had an ice storm that knocked our electricity out.  I had a Chevette car with a diesel motor and I had a 120 hp 3 phase motor.  We mounted a wheel with a tire to the induction motor and jacked the car up and placed the tire that was mounted to the 3 phase motor against one of the rear tires on the Chevette.  The motor was a 460 volt 3 phase motor so I only used 2 of the three wires and ran the output through a transformer to get 240 volts. Then I added a large bank of oil filled capacitors.  I adjusted the speed of the Chevette engine to give me 60 hertz. I connected the system to my home and we had electricity all night...only had to add more diesel fuel the next morning...worked great!  The trick to using an induction motor as an alternator is to exceed the rated RPM of the motor and add enough capacitors.  This was before I had a smart phone to video the system....TRUE STORY!

author: @therealjammit
text: If you power down the generator while connected to a load the initial magnetization of the core will be zero. If that happens disconnect the load and don't spin the generator (static) and temporarily apply a "AA" battery to it. The battery will leave a residual magnetic field that will be a starting magnetic field when you spin the generator up. To help prevent demagnetizing the field put two silicon diodes in anti parallel (anode of one to the cathode of two, and the cathode of one to the anode of two) and put the diodes in series with one of the load wires. The diodes will disconnect the load when the voltage drops down to either plus or minus 0.6v and leave a tiny bit of residual magnetic flux in the generator core. The small 0.6 drop won't be a serious loss at 120/220 volts AC. The generator works better with more capacitance. You can use polarized capacitors but you have to take another step before hooking them up. You take two of the same capacitance and hook their negatives together. The negatives are insulated and not used. The remaining two positive leads are used as if they aren't polarized and can be used on AC. The capacitance rating is halved but you now have a non-polarized capacitor.

The effect is to increase torque when the motor is started. The auxiliary winding is disconnected by a centrifugal switch when the motor comes up to speed. Is it too much to ask that your explanations are accurate?

author: @terryharris3393
text: Induction motors have no brushes. The electric current in the rotor is induced from the magnetic field set up in the stator through gaps between the rotor and stator due to Faraday’s law and a torque is produced in the rotor to oppose the induction due to Lenz’s law. The motor you are describing is a DC motor not an induction motor. It’s fun to watch you tinker but frustrating to see you Bollix the science.

text: Tip of the day when playing with ready made motors  you will need to know .some motors have allouy windings looking like copper wire . even coated the same colour . What you will find is when you try to energize and parts such as the stata or rota com it will fail . in most cases the armature  will have copper wire but fields are made of allouy .metals , what the chinese do is attach 2inches of copper wire to the ends of there cleaner and washer motors with crimps hid in insulation , so if you grab the motor and clean of some insulation it shows to be copper ,. In truth this is done because of heat and currents traveling  across  the connections . when the com spins and brush gear make contact frequencies of 216hz will be made  per second while the motor pulses on and off .The faster the spin the higher the frequency the less current drawn .When under load brush motors draw more current as they slow under load .So the crafty sods attach real copper to the fields . Now when you try feeding the motor with a voltage to create a magnetic field all you get back is just a few volts .. so take off the brushes and remove the bearings for future projects and bin it , Some washer motors have the same set up and want make a bean , so don;t wast time and check first for copper .

replies.author: @Merlin3189
replies.text:
Don't worry about that.  It's the power output you want.  AC can easily be transformed to other voltages - by a transformer (the clue is in the name!)
What RM-S always forgets to mention is the power, which is the rate of useful work it can do.  50 V at 1 A is 50 W and that can be transformed to 250 V at 0.2 A which is still 50 W and is useful.    If your generator got  250 V, but only 4 mA, that might sound better, but is only 1 W of useful power.

His emergency battery with  0.3 V at 40 mA is 0.012 W and of dubious value even for charging a phone.  Just think how your phone discharges even if you don't make or receive any calls:  mine might last 48 hours like that.  Lets be generous and say it could do 100 hours on a charge, if I don't use it.  My phone requires about 5 W hours for a full charge, so 0.012 W will take about 400 hours!   
But it's not useless!  I can make dozens of calls a day on a full charge, so 5 - 10 hours charging might just give me a single call, provided the phone was off the rest of the time.

replies.author: @brianbaird4573
replies.text:
You have to use NON-polarized caps for this. If you use the ones out of power supplies, they will not like being charged backwards during the other side of the sine wave. They will either waste too much energy and the gen simply won't work, or they could blow up. 
You need a RUN capacitor from an electric motor. Or get one from a air conditioning service shop. Or on Amazon. Make sure to use a cap with a voltage rating close to double the voltage you intend to generate, or it will either get hot or explode.

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text:
He did. The capacitor is discharged at the beginning. The tiny residual magnetism in the motor generates a tiny current, which begins to charge the capacitor. Then feedback causes both the magnetic field and electrostatic charge to build until useable power is generated. 

There is an older book, called 'Electrotechnology', whose author's name I cannot remember, but it was being used in Britain during the 1970's as a base for teaching that subject in universities and colleges. It explains the process, and much more. There will be plenty of other sensible  resources, online and in libraries, which also explain everything to you. 

Beware of the 'free energy' and 'perpetual motion' claims; they are wrong, misinformed or scams.

author: @gaionaus
text: In my opinion, for wind turbines, but ONLY for charging batteries this will probably be ok. Asynchronous motors as generators in general (e.x. fitted to a diesel engine ) have issues. They are only useful if they are grid tied!  This is why industry only uses them as generators in GRID tied wind turbines. They don't work reliably offgrid and specially under induction loads. You don't want a generator that suddenly looses it's field and stops when the load changes ... Anyway, a really HUGE flywheel adds easily some "balance" to the system and with the help of a microcontroller  you can make an "acceptable" generator...  The best way to make an asynchronous motor generator is to change or modify its rotor WITH a field rotor with windings and machine slips and add brushes for the dc field voltage. That way can the motor can be converted to a real offgrid generator without any of the above issues.

author: @dcawkwell
text:
Here is an example of how to calculate the capacitance needed I got this example of wiki. It goes up if you need to draw a load from the generator.

As an example, consider the use of a 10 hp, 1760 r/min, 440 V, three-phase induction motor as an asynchronous generator. The full-load current of the motor is 10 A and the full-load power factor is 0.8.

Required capacitance per phase if capacitors are connected in delta:

Apparent power S = √3 E I = 1.73 × 440 × 10 = 7612 VA
Active power P = S cos θ = 7612 × 0.8 = 6090 W
Reactive power Q = {\displaystyle {\sqrt {S^{2}-P^{2}}}}{\sqrt  {S^{2}-P^{2}}} = 4567 VAR
For a machine to run as an asynchronous generator, capacitor bank must supply minimum 4567 / 3 phases = 1523 VAR per phase. Voltage per capacitor is 440 V because capacitors are connected in delta.

Capacitive current Ic = Q/E = 1523/440 = 3.46 A
Capacitive reactance per phase Xc = E/Ic = 127 Ω
Minimum capacitance per phase:

C = 1 / (2*π*f*Xc) = 1 / (2 * 3.141 * 60 * 127) = 21 microfarads.
If the load also absorbs reactive power, capacitor bank must be increased in size to compensate.

Prime mover speed should be used to generate frequency of 60 Hz:

Typically, slip should be similar to full-load value when machine is running as motor, but negative (generator operation):

if Ns = 1800, one can choose N=Ns+40 rpm
Required prime mover speed N = 1800 + 40 = 1840 rpm.

author: @charlieangkor8649
text: To calculate the capacitor, you measure the inductance of the motor and calculate the capacity for resonant frequency of the mains. But I think it's a bit off. First, the inductance of turning motor is different than when it's standing, because you are stealing the magnetic field of the coil under its hands and turning it upside down which is not supposed to happen in an ordinary fixed coil. You could measure the inductance with a coil meter while you are running the motor slightly above nominal rpms driven by an electric drill, but that 1 volt AC from remanent magneticm may throw your coil meter off. Second, the rotor core is nonlinear and saturates in normal operation, which lowers the actual running inductance. Third, it has to run a bit above the mains frequency instead of a bit below. Really, this "self limiting" capability you are talking about in case of wind turbine is of a very bad quality. Because if the turbine is pushing hard and your load consumes only so much energy, the remaining energy has to be dumped somewhere. The core oversaturates and the magnetzation losses heat it up a lot. You are basically heating up the rotor. Which is not good, the motor gets hot and the efficiency is bad even when this doesn't happen. Permanent magnet motors or even car alternators are much much better more efficient generators than this mind blowing intellectual curiosity. If you use a smaller capacitor, the motor will resonate at higher rpms, produce more voltage and be harder to start, run colder and have more efficiency. If you use big capacitor, it will be easy to start, start at low RPM but will heat a lot efficiency will be worse. Extreme capacitors may not work at all because something in the complex dynamic of this system gets thrown off so much a negative resistance is not possible anymore.

author: @charlieangkor8649
text: I even melted steel using a human pedalled induction motor as a generator (without modification of the motor). Google Twibright Exciter. There is a video of electric arcs. The motor is a small 3-phase one (fits into a hand) and when under load and disconnected, with voltage limiting disabled, the resulting voltage spike sometimes broke through 1000 V rectifier diodes. Very dangerous. But thanks to this, it was extremely easy to draw and hold an electric arc. The steel I melted was a steel paper clip. The efficiency was however rather poor. Combined mechanical and electrical efficiency I estimated as only 50%. This motor is very easy to voltage limit. If the voltage exceeds preset value, you simply short circuit the output. The core demagnetizes (dumps the small magnetic energy into the internal resistance) and the motor has to restart the self-induction magnetization cycle. If this happens as you pedal, it feels like it's "slipping through". The three-phase rectifier produces a beautifully stable DC voltage for the arc. Another funny thing is if this thing is overloaded, it won't start and the generator will freely turn through. As soon as you connect an appropriate load, the magnetic field boots up and the motor will start braking and producing energy. You can spin up the bicycle wheel to crazy revs and then remove the short and the machine will massively dump energy into the load. Depends on the load. It doesn't want to allow you to increase the revs much above the resonant frequency. So any kind of overrevs are brutally braked and dumped as electric energy. If you have a good short circuiting overvoltage regulator though, this kind of "flywheel compulsator dump" will just result in the thing cyclically slipping through and rebooting.

author: @brianspencer6397
text:
'Motors as Generators in Micro-Hydro Power' by Nigel Smith is probably available thru your local library, and gives a comprehensive guide to setting up an induction generator, selection of capacitors for induction generators, with a good description of the process to get the motor characteristics to calculate the capacitance, and a number of sample calculations to guide you in the (not overly difficult) process.

The capacitor is used to cancel out the reactive (inductive) current that the motor draws, apparently. This reactive current is relatively constant over the range of power outputs from the motor. A fair approximation of the required value can be found by (a) measuring the current drawn by the motor at no load, preferably with the cooling ducts blocked, to reduce the actual work being done by the motor. That current is mainly magnetising (inductive) current, with only a small 'true power' current driving the reduced windage, bearing friction, and low iron and copper resistive losses. (Some of the measured current is still driving resistive losses in the motor, but at no load, and with the cooling duct blocked, these losses are minimised, and most of the current will be reactive.)

Then, (b) determine the reactance of the motor to draw that current. XL = V / I. That gives the approximate reactance the capacitor needs to have to compensate for the motor reactance. (c) The capacitance can now be found from C(uF) = 10^6 / (2 x pi x f x Xc). 

Example: a 1kW single phase motor draws 2.3A from a 230V supply at no-load. The motor reactance is approximately (230 / 2.3) ohms = 100 ohms. The capacitor that will also have approximately 100 ohms reactance at 50 Hz is.... (10^6 / (2 x pi x 50 x 100) = 31.8uF. That's the starting point for setting up the capacitor; it /will/ require a slightly larger capacitance to hit the 'sweet spot' for best operation.

For 3 phase machines, refer to Nigel Smith's book. The principle is the same, but the capacitors may be connected in either star or delta, and the capacitances and voltage ratings are different for each of those cases.

The capacitor is not a motor-starting type - these are rated for intermittent operation only, and often less than the motor voltage. (That's another story, though.) A polyester or other 'motor-run' capacitor is required, and should be rated at a higher voltage that the output voltage of the machine in operation. (Switching off a large load can cause a 50% surge in output volts in some machines!)

You know nothing of efficiency and electricity. Most motors are highly efficient, they'll have efficiency ratings right on the nameplate. Usually 85% and higher. 

You clearly understand nothing about torque and physics. Newton's laws state an object at rest tends to stay at rest. Which means you need to add a bit of force just to get something moving. Refrigeration and hvac, or any compressor, takes an incredible amount of energy to force a phase change of a gas via compression. author: @travishanson166
text:
On my 1980 Honda cm200t , it took 55,000 micro farads (on dc output, voltage is 7.7 volts and doesn't drop when lights come on) to get the charger to work without a battery. 

On a generac induction generator (whose internal capacitor was bad) I was able to use 6x 23 farad(maybe micro farad, I dont recall)  600 volt hydrogen lighting starting capacitors to get it to fire back up.  Capacitors were plugged into the extra outlet with the load into the other.  The capacitors burned out after a day, but when I would go over 1000 watts, the field collapsed until the load fell into the range of the generator. 

I think if I had more farad than I needed, the capacitors would last.

Or just order the replacement part. 

I was out of power for three days and I wanted to make coffee and watch tv but the free generator wasnt working.   So I just started trying stuff after disassembly and reassembling didnt work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: 23 farads is a lot mate likely to be microfards thogh i think more like 200 microfards would be better

replies.author: @travishanson166
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah I was probably only at around 122 mf,  I'll order some modern caps if the replacement part isn't available, and maybe do some more experiments.   The thing needs a new fuel tank, possibly a new diode on the rotor.  (Probably the reason to need extra capacitance) .

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @travishanson166  remember dont use electrolitic capacitors.  Film capacitors are likely to get hot.  Basically, youll want a run capacitor  that can handle  ..... ill say at least 3 times the peek voltage of what your working with, unless someone has a better recommendation.

replies.author: @travishanson166
replies.text:
@kreynolds1123 that's pretty much what I was thinking.  The ones I was using were 600v but I dont think they were built for the duty cycle, plus I was probably under the necessary farads by about 80 minimum.  They were also of the "outlawed" type. When I try it again I will take notes and see how long things last with a load on.  I'd like to get one of those wattage tracking devices instead of doing the math.   If I can record the voltage swings and peak wattage before the field collapses I can fine tune the necessary capacitance for the duty. 

I think they stopped making parts for that particular generator, so getting specific replacements might be difficult.   But it's mostly about learning how and why it all works than anything.   Then accepting any free generator that someone says is broken lol.

author: @mwm2929
text: Car alternators use induction type rotors and are able to generate quite efficiently just by connecting a capacitor to the field windings.  Around a hundred amps out and at a regulated voltage if you use a battery.  The only current limiting factor is the field current so you can use a supercapacitor pack around 100F to get the amps up rather quickly.  These things are cheaper and smaller since their incorporation into the RC vehicle market.

replies.author: @travishanson166
replies.text:
Need a load cell or an electronic switch that cuts throttle when voltage changes by a particular amount or rpm trigger.  

I have a coleman generator that only has 1 speed, and it's only happy when it's got a load on. Sounds like its going to come apart every time the air compressor shuts off. 

Need a voltage limiting device that only comes on when there is net wattage available.  Think a light bulb that goes dim when a fan comes on, then bright when the fan turns off.   The draw would be 0 if load is used elsewhere,  but up to a few hundred watts when load drops off.  Maybe just a 250 watt incandescent bulb would do the job without fancy electronics.  

I'm contemplating an $8 arduino and an automotive relay with a lock solenoid to sense either rpm or voltage delta to cut throttle to idle on the colman.     Otherwise it's around $200 to adapt a throttle control off a Honda.  


I'll be using the coleman again today, I plan to try the light bulb idea to see if it will keep it sounding healthy.  If I had a tach I could ignore the sound, provided it was running at rated speed.

replies.author: @PeterMilanovski
replies.text:
@travishanson166 probably a simple solution would be to add a bunch of diode's in series so that they turn on at a specific voltage, the diodes will remain off until the voltage goes over what you want it to and begin to conduct to a light bulb or two to keep a load on it.. it's automatic and it's cheap! Just size the diode's for the voltage and current that they are going to be working with, add up each of the voltage drop from each diode till you get to where you want to be at, if you don't want to light things up, you can always use a high powered resistor to dial in how much load you need to keep it stable. The light is great for testing as you can see when the diode's are turning on but once you have it worked out, swap out the light for a resistor in a similar resistance.. I know it's not as cool as using a microcontroller but it should work and it doesn't need power to operate! 
Hope this helps 🙂

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
While the mains freqency in many parts of the world is 50hz, in the US it is 60hz. A permanent magnet motor, is a synchronous type motor with its synchronous speed depending on the number of pairs of poles in the field coils. In the US  a synchronous motor will spin at (60hz aka cycles a second) × (60 seconds a minute)/(number of poles/2). 

So in the US, with 2 poles making a pair in the stator, the synchronous speed is 3600 rpm.  2 pole pairs (4 poles) = 1800 rpm. 4 pole pairs = 900 rpm.  In say the UK that would be 50hz ×60 seconds/(2/2)=3000 rpm. 50×60/(4/2)=1500,  and 50×60/(8/2)=750 rpm respectively. 

Now induction motors are asynchronous type motors and they require a difference in rotating speed between the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field in the electromagnetic field coils, and the rotating speed of the rotor.  Induction motors are generally rated at about 5% slower than  synchronous, in the US (3600rpm becomes 3420rpm, 1800rpm become 1710rpm).  Too much slip (over 5%) causes excessive heating in the rotor and field coils.

Grid excitation: If you pluged an asynchronous induction motor into the wall and spin it faster than its synchronous speed, then instead of drawing power from the grid, it will generate electrical power pumping that power into the grid.  Because of the induced currents inside the rotor cause heat in the rotor,  you should not go any faster than about 5% faster slip than synchronous with the grid. For exampke, in the UK  with 1 pole pair field winding motor  50×60/(2/2) × (slip factor 1.05)=3150rpm. 

Self excited inductlion motors store "reactive power" in the capacitors with the inductance and capacitance acting as a resonate capacitor inductor tank circuit.  The resonate freqency (aka syncronouse speed) of the tank  circuit in the most simplist terms, is a function of capacitor and inductance  1/(2×pi×sqr(L×C)).  Adding resistance in the coils and load complicate the formula. 

Now it will continue to generate power as long as you dont try to pull  too much power. Causing it to loose its self excitation. I don't have an equation for that.

replies.author: @boblewis5558
replies.text:
The big difference with an alternator of course is that a) it IS a generator to start with b) it STILL has the potential problem of being brushed, albeit just to slip rings so "slightly" less wear. Some people may be tempted to over-rev an alternator to try to increase the output volts (and current) but it's dodgy at best. The better approach is to use a 24 volt truck alternator run at peak revs (for its rating) and run the high power 24v/ 40-60 Amp (1.4 kWatt) into an inverter with a 24volt battery (or two 12v batteries in series) to smooth the DC from the rectified 3 phase alternator output. I have seen several setups like this for off grid power. 


The main problems you will face are achieving sufficient rotor speed to produce maximum torque and output power whilst also achieving an efficient power transfer at the required speed without stalling the rotor at low wind speeds or the turbine having insufficient torque to start properly especially when the output is loaded. Electronic controls are useful here to both unload the output AND unload the rotor by not applying slip ring current until OVER a specific rotational turbine speed and not applying output load until that rotational speed is above a stable lower limit (different for VAWT & HAWT- higher for the latter). It is unlikely that maximum power will be achieved with such a system until winds of OVER 20mph (30kph, 8-9 msec^-1) or even greater are consistently blowing. Hence the need for an efficient turbine design either VAWT or traditional  large bladed HAWT. Remember that VWT designs are potentially capable of withstanding typhoon level winds but HAWT designs have a MUCH lower limit.

replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering this is very similar to what nikola tesla was talking about in the polyphase chapters of his inventions researches and writings. this is why to his dying days he claimed his discovery of the rotating magnetic field was his most significant discovery. he speaks of synchronous and lag motors with different combinations of poles, alternators and "condensers", capacitors.

 chapter viii is entitled single circuit, self-starting synchronizing motors. these circuits use a dead resistance, an inductive resistance and mechanical switches to start a motor. its not until chapter xxi we encounter capacitances. entitled 'motor with a condenser in the armature circuit', it begins by noting these are the type he used in his high frequency work. i think basically what is said is that the capacitors emulate the mechanical switch. he goes on to describe in more detail these types of motors and i would love to see you do a video on the topic.
